s0001.idiomatic: Yu of Xia bore the name Wenming.

s0002.literal: Yu's father was called Gun; Gun's father was called Emperor Zhuanxu; Zhuanxu's father was called Changyi; Changyi's father was the Yellow Emperor.
s0002.idiomatic: Yu's father was Gun; Gun's father was Emperor Zhuanxu; Zhuanxu's father was Changyi; Changyi's father was the Yellow Emperor.

s0003.literal: Yu was the Yellow Emperor's great-great-grandson and Emperor Zhuanxu's grandson.
s0003.idiomatic: Yu was a great-great-grandson of the Yellow Emperor and a grandson of Emperor Zhuanxu.

s0004.literal: Yu's great-grandfather Changyi and father Gun both did not attain the imperial throne; they were subjects.
s0004.idiomatic: Neither Yu's great-grandfather Changyi nor his father Gun ever held the throne; both served merely as ministers.

s0005.literal: In Emperor Yao's time the vast flood swelled to heaven; vastly it embraced mountains and overflowed hills; the common people worried over it.
s0005.idiomatic: Under Emperor Yao the floodwaters rose to the sky, spread around the peaks, and overtopped the hills, and the people lived in dread of them.

s0006.literal: Yao sought one able to regulate the waters; all ministers and presidents of the Four Peaks said Gun could do it.
s0006.idiomatic: Yao looked for someone who could manage the flood; the officials and the lords of the Four Peaks all named Gun.

s0007.literal: Yao said, "Gun as a man defies commands and ruins kin groups; not acceptable."
s0007.idiomatic: Yao replied, "Gun disobeys orders and harms his own people. I cannot use him."

s0008.literal: The Four Peaks said, "Among their peers none is worthier than Gun; we beg the Emperor to try him."
s0008.idiomatic: The Four Peaks answered, "No one in their ranks surpasses Gun; we ask you to give him a trial."

s0009.literal: Thereupon Yao heeded the Four Peaks and employed Gun to regulate the waters.
s0009.idiomatic: Yao yielded and put Gun in charge of the flood works.

s0010.literal: After nine years still the waters did not subside; the task was not completed.
s0010.idiomatic: Nine years passed, the flood never relented, and the project failed.

s0011.literal: Thereupon Emperor Yao then sought another man and obtained Shun in his stead.
s0011.idiomatic: Emperor Yao began looking again and found Shun.

s0012.literal: Shun was promoted and employed; he exercised the Son of Heaven's government in proxy and went on tours of inspection.
s0012.idiomatic: Shun was raised to high office, administered affairs in the Son of Heaven's name, and toured the realm.

s0013.literal: On tour he observed Gun's regulation of the waters—no achievement—and then struck Gun dead on Mount Yu.
s0013.idiomatic: On his rounds he saw that Gun had achieved nothing with the waters, so he had Gun executed on Mount Yu.

s0014.literal: All under Heaven considered Shun's punishment right.
s0014.idiomatic: Throughout the realm people judged Shun's sentence just.

s0015.literal: Thereupon Shun raised Gun's son Yu and had him continue Gun's task.
s0015.idiomatic: Shun then promoted Gun's son Yu to carry on his father's work.

s0016.literal: Yao passed away; Emperor Shun questioned the Four Peaks, saying, "Is there anyone who can complete and beautify Yao's undertakings—appoint him to office?"
s0016.idiomatic: When Yao died, Emperor Shun asked the Four Peaks whether anyone could bring Yao's projects to fruition and hold office.

s0017.literal: All said, "Lord Yu as Minister of Works can complete and beautify Yao's achievements."
s0017.idiomatic: They said, "Lord Yu, as Minister of Works, could finish what Yao began."

s0018.idiomatic: Shun exclaimed, "Well said—it shall be so."

s0019.literal: He charged Yu: "You—level the waters and earth; only this—exert yourself."
s0019.idiomatic: He ordered Yu: "You shall bring the land and waters to order; give it your whole strength."

s0020.literal: Yu bowed touching head to ground and yielded to Xie, Prince Millet, and Gao Yao.
s0020.idiomatic: Yu kowtowed and declined in favor of Xie, Hou Ji, and Gao Yao.

s0021.idiomatic: Shun told him, "Go—all that matters is that you do the work."

s0023.literal: Yu as a person was quick, responsive, and able in diligence.
s0023.idiomatic: Yu was quick-witted, responsive, and tirelessly industrious.

s0024.literal: His virtue did not deviate; his benevolence was approachable; his words were trustworthy.
s0024.idiomatic: He kept to the moral path, drew others with kindness, and spoke so that men could rely on him.

s0025.literal: His voice was a pitch-pipe; his body was a measure; proportion issued from them.
s0025.idiomatic: His voice matched the tuning tubes, his bearing matched the plumb line, and he carried himself with measured grace.

s0026.literal: Earnest, earnest; solemn, solemn—he was warp and he was woof.
s0026.idiomatic: Steadfast and grave, he set the pattern others followed.

s0027.literal: Yu then together with Yi and Hou Ji received the Emperor's commands; he ordered the lords and commoners to raise work gangs to spread earth, marched along mountains marking trees, and fixed the high mountains and great rivers.
s0027.idiomatic: Yu, with Yi and Hou Ji, carried out the Son of Heaven's orders, mobilized nobles and commoners to shift soil, blazed trails along ridges and marked timbers, and charted every major height and watercourse.

s0028.literal: Yu grieved that his forebear and father Gun had not completed the work and suffered execution; thereupon he wore out his body and burned his thoughts, dwelt outside thirteen years, passed his gate without daring to enter.
s0028.idiomatic: He mourned that Gun had died unfinished yet under sentence of death, so he drove himself until body and mind were spent, spent thirteen years away from home, and even passed his own door without stopping.

s0029.literal: He made thin his clothing and food; he extended filial devotion toward ghosts and spirits.
s0029.idiomatic: He lived plainly in dress and diet and showed the spirits the utmost reverence.

s0030.literal: He made low his palace chambers; he expended effort on ditches and channels.
s0030.idiomatic: He kept his halls modest and poured labor into drains and canals.

s0031.literal: On land he rode in a carriage; on water he rode in a boat; in mud he rode a sledge; in mountains he rode a palanquin.
s0031.idiomatic: He traveled by carriage on dry ground, by boat on water, by sledge in mire, and by litter in the hills.

s0032.literal: Left the marking-cord and line, right the compass and square; he bore the four seasons to open the nine provinces, open the nine roads, bank the nine marshes, measure the nine mountains.
s0032.idiomatic: Plumb line in his left hand, square in his right, he followed the seasons, opened the nine provinces, linked the nine highways, diked the nine marshes, and surveyed the nine ranges.

s0033.literal: He ordered Yi to give the multitude rice, plantable in low damp places.
s0033.idiomatic: He told Yi to distribute rice seed for the people to sow in the wet lowlands.

s0034.literal: He ordered Prince Millet to give the multitude hard-to-obtain food.
s0034.idiomatic: He told Hou Ji to supply food where famine threatened.

s0035.literal: When food was scarce, he adjusted surplus to mutual aid so as to equalize the lords.
s0035.idiomatic: Where supplies ran short, he moved surplus from one region to another so that no domain starved alone.

s0036.literal: Yu then proceeded to survey each land for what it should offer as tribute and for the convenience of mountains and rivers.
s0036.idiomatic: He then assessed what each region could yield in tribute and how mountains and rivers could serve transport.

s0037.idiomatic: Yu began his survey in Jizhou.

s0038.literal: Jizhou: already loaded at Hukou, regulated Liang and Qi.
s0038.idiomatic: In Jizhou he finished work at Hukou and brought order to the Liang and Qi ranges.

s0039.literal: Already repaired Taiyuan, reaching the south of Mount Yue.
s0039.idiomatic: He restored Taiyuan and continued south of Mount Yue.

s0040.literal: Tanhuai achieved merit, reaching Heng and Zhang.
s0040.idiomatic: He completed the Tanhuai basin and carried the work to the Heng and Zhang rivers.

s0041.idiomatic: The soil was pale, loose earth.

s0042.literal: Revenue highest of highest with admixture; fields middle of middle; Chang and Wei already followed; the great plain was formed.
s0042.idiomatic: Its tax was rated top grade with some second-grade tracts; its fields were middling; the Chang and Wei ran true, and the great alluvial plain emerged.

s0043.idiomatic: Coastal peoples sent furs and hides.

s0044.literal: Flanking right Jieshi, entering the sea.
s0044.idiomatic: Tribute went by way of Jieshi on the right and reached the sea.

s0045.literal: Ji and He were Yanzhou: the nine rivers already had their courses; Leixia already was a marsh; Yong and Ju met together; mulberry soil already had silkworms; then the people could descend from heights and dwell on earth.
s0045.idiomatic: Between the Ji and the Yellow River lay Yanzhou: the Nine Streams were channeled, Leixia became a lake, the Yong and Ju joined their waters, silkworms returned to the mulberry lands, and people could leave the heights for the plain.

s0046.literal: Its soil was black and rich; grass luxuriant, trees straight.
s0046.idiomatic: The ground was dark fertile loam with thick grass and tall straight timber.

s0047.literal: Fields lower middle; revenue fixed; after thirteen years then uniform.
s0047.idiomatic: Fields ranked low-middle; the levy was set at the lowest regular grade until, after thirteen years of reclamation, it matched other districts.

s0048.literal: Its tribute lacquer and silk; its baskets patterned weave.
s0048.idiomatic: It sent lacquer and silk in tribute, and woven figured cloth in baskets.

s0049.literal: Floated on Ji and Ta, communicating with He.
s0049.idiomatic: Shipments floated down the Ji and the Ta to join the Yellow River.

s0050.literal: Sea and Dai were Qingzhou: Yuyi already subdued; Wei and Zi had their courses.
s0050.idiomatic: Between the sea and Mount Dai lay Qingzhou: the Yuyi tribes were pacified, and the Wei and Zi were channeled.

s0051.literal: Its soil white loam; seashore broad marshes; its fields saline.
s0051.idiomatic: The soil was pale loam along a coast of wide tidal flats, much of it salt-encrusted.

s0052.literal: Fields upper lower; revenue middle upper.
s0052.idiomatic: Fields ranked high among the lower grades; the tax was upper-middle.

s0053.literal: Its tribute salt and fine cloth; sea things various; Dai's valleys silk, hemp, lead, pine, strange stones; Laiyi herded; its baskets mulberry silk.
s0053.idiomatic: Tribute included salt, fine linen, a miscellany of sea produce, silk and hemp from the Dai valleys, lead, pine, and odd stones; the Lai people sent pasture goods and silkworm baskets.

s0054.literal: Floated on Wen, communicating with Ji.
s0054.idiomatic: It went by the Wen and linked to the Ji.

s0055.literal: Sea, Dai, and Huai were Xuzhou: Huai and Yi were regulated; Meng and Yu were cultivated.
s0055.idiomatic: Between sea, Dai, and Huai lay Xuzhou: the Huai and Yi were tamed, and the Meng and Yu ranges were opened to the plough.

s0056.literal: The great marsh already settled; the eastern plain reached level.
s0056.idiomatic: Daye marsh was diked and the eastern lowlands lay smooth.

s0057.literal: Its soil red sticky loam; grass and trees gradually encased.
s0057.idiomatic: The earth was sticky red clay, slowly clothing itself again in grass and wood.

s0058.literal: Its fields upper middle; revenue middle middle.
s0058.idiomatic: Fields ranked second grade; the tax was middling.

s0059.literal: Tribute earth of five colors; summer pheasant plumes from Yu's valley; lone paulownia from Yiyang; floating chime-stones from the Si bank; pearls and fish from Huaiyi; its baskets black fine silk.
s0059.idiomatic: It sent five-colored clays, pheasant tails from the Yu valley, lone paulownia from Yiyang, floating lithophones from the Si, pearls and fish from the Huai tribes, and baskets of fine dark silk.

s0060.literal: Floated on Huai and Si, communicating with He.
s0060.idiomatic: Shipments moved on the Huai and Si to reach the Yellow River.

s0061.literal: Huai and sea were Yangzhou: Pengli already settled; where the yang birds dwell.
s0061.idiomatic: Between the Huai and the sea lay Yangzhou: Lake Pengli was stabilized and became a haunt for wintering birds.

s0062.literal: Three rivers already entered; Zhenze reached stability.
s0062.idiomatic: The three great channels poured in, and Lake Tai was calmed.

s0063.idiomatic: Bamboo groves for arrows flourished far and wide.

s0064.literal: Its grass only young; its trees only tall; its soil mud.
s0064.idiomatic: Grasses were tender, trees towered, and the soil was mire.

s0065.literal: Fields lowest lowest; revenue lowest upper upper mixed.
s0065.idiomatic: Fields were the poorest grade; the levy was the top of the bottom tier with some higher admixture.

s0066.literal: Tribute metals three grades, jade, kun stone, bamboo arrows; teeth, hides, feathers, yak-hair; island barbarians grass-cloth; its baskets woven shells; its bundles oranges and pomelo granted tribute.
s0066.idiomatic: Tribute listed three grades of metal, jade, fine stone, bamboo arrows, ivory, hides, feathers, and yak-tail; island peoples sent plant-fiber dress; baskets held patterned shells; bundles of citrus were granted as occasional gifts.

s0067.literal: Evened Jiang and sea, communicating Huai and Si.
s0067.idiomatic: He linked the Yangtze to the sea and opened routes to the Huai and Si.

s0068.literal: Jing and south of Heng were Jingzhou: Jiang and Han paid court to the sea.
s0068.idiomatic: From Mount Jing to south of Mount Heng lay Jingzhou: the Yangtze and Han flowed as tributaries to the sea.

s0069.literal: Nine Jiang very central; Tuo and Cen already channeled; cloud-earth and dream reached regulation.
s0069.idiomatic: The nine channels of the middle Yangtze were cleared, the Tuo and Cen confined, and the Yunmeng marshes brought under control.

s0071.literal: Fields lower middle; revenue upper lower.

s0072.literal: Tribute feathers, yak-hair, teeth, hides, metals three grades, oak, catalpa, cypress, grindstones, whetstones, arrowheads, cinnabar, bamboo and wood of Jun and Lu, three states sent their named tribute, wrapped calamus and thatch, baskets black and red, pearl strings; nine Jiang entered great tortoise granted.
s0072.idiomatic: Tribute ran to feathers, furs, ivory, hides, three grades of ore, hardwoods, whetstones, cinnabar, specialty bamboos—three chiefdoms each sent their specialties—wrapped iris and thatch, baskets of dark silk and strings of pearls, and from the nine streams the great tortoise as imperial gift.

s0073.literal: Floated on Jiang, Tuo, Cen, to Han, crossed to Luo, reaching south He.
s0073.idiomatic: Goods floated on the Yangtze, Tuo, and Cen to the Han, then were portaged to the Luo and the southern Yellow River.

s0074.literal: Jing and He were Yuzhou: Yi, Luo, Chan, and Jian already entered He; Xingbo already settled; channeled He marsh, spread Mingdu.
s0074.idiomatic: Between the Jing mountains and the Yellow River lay Yuzhou: the Yi, Luo, Chan, and Jian were led into the main stream, Lake Xingbo was diked, the He marsh was opened, and Mingdu was flooded out.

s0075.literal: Its soil soil; lower soil loam.
s0075.idiomatic: The upland was loess; the low ground heavier clay.

s0076.literal: Fields middle upper; revenue mixed upper middle.
s0076.idiomatic: Fields were second grade; the tax blended upper and middle rates.

s0077.literal: Tribute lacquer, silk, fine cloth, hemp; its baskets fine floss; granted tribute chime-stone ore.
s0077.idiomatic: It sent lacquer, silk, fine linen, and kudzu fiber in baskets of fine batting, with lithophone ore as an occasional gift.

s0078.idiomatic: Shipments floated down the Luo to the Yellow River.

s0079.literal: South of Hua and Black Water were Liangzhou: Min and Bo already cultivated; Tuo and Cen already channeled; Cai and Meng journeyed level; He yi reached merit.
s0079.idiomatic: South of Mount Hua to the Black Water was Liangzhou: the Min and Bo were farmed, the Tuo and Cen confined, the Cai and Meng ranges cleared, and the He tribes brought in order.

s0081.literal: Fields lower upper; revenue lower middle three mixed.
s0081.idiomatic: Fields ranked low among the high grades; the levy shifted among three lower-middle levels.

s0082.literal: Tribute jade, iron, silver, carved bells, lithophones, bears, brown bears, foxes, wildcats, woven hides.
s0082.idiomatic: It yielded fine jade, iron, silver, hammered metal, lithophones, and pelts of bear, brown bear, fox, and wildcat.

s0083.literal: West Qin by Huan came; floated on Qian, crossed Mian, entered Wei, crossed He.
s0083.idiomatic: From West Qin the route followed the Huan, then floated on the Qian, was portaged to the Mian, joined the Wei, and crossed to the Yellow River.

s0084.literal: Black Water west of He was Yongzhou: weak water already west; Jing joined Wei's north bank.
s0084.idiomatic: West of the Black Water to the Yellow River was Yongzhou: the Weak River was turned westward, and the Jing joined the Wei at its bend.

s0085.literal: Qi and Ju already followed; Feng water shared the same.
s0085.idiomatic: The Qi and Ju obeyed their channels, and the Feng mingled where appointed.

s0086.literal: Jing and Qi already journeyed; Zhongnan, Dunwu reached Niaoshu.
s0086.idiomatic: The Jing and Qi ranges were cleared from Zhongnan and Dunwu to Niaoshu.

s0087.literal: High plain and low marsh reached merit, reaching Duye.
s0087.idiomatic: High moors and sunken bogs were drained as far as Duye.

s0088.literal: Three Perils already measured; Three Miao greatly ordered.
s0088.idiomatic: The Sanwei region was surveyed, and the Sanmiao tribes were brought into line.

s0090.literal: Fields highest highest; revenue middle lower.
s0090.idiomatic: Fields were the finest grade; the tax was set middle-lower to reflect distance.

s0091.idiomatic: It sent jade, fine stone, and carnelian.

s0092.literal: Floated on Jishi, reaching Longmen west He, meeting at Wei's north bank.
s0092.idiomatic: Shipments floated from Jishi to Longmen on the western Yellow River and gathered at the Wei's bend.

s0093.literal: Woven hides Kunlun, Xizhi, Qusou; western Rong already ordered.
s0093.idiomatic: Tribute furs came from Kunlun, Xizhi, and Qusou, and the western Rong were enrolled.

s0094.literal: He opened nine mountains: Qian and Qi reaching Jingshan, crossing He.
s0094.idiomatic: He traced nine mountain chains: from Qian and Qi to Jingshan, then across the Yellow River.

s0095.idiomatic: From Hukou and Leishou to Taiyue.

s0096.idiomatic: From Dizhu and Xicheng to Wangwu.

s0097.literal: Taihang, Changshan reaching Jieshi, entering the sea.
s0097.idiomatic: From Taihang and Hengshan to Jieshi and into the sea.

s0098.literal: West Qin, Zhuyu, Niaoshu reaching Taihua.
s0098.idiomatic: From West Qin, Zhuyu, and Niaoshu to Taihua.

s0099.idiomatic: From Xiong'er, Waifang, and Tongbai to Fuwei.

s0100.idiomatic: He opened the Bozhong range to Jingshan.

s0102.literal: South of Mount Min's range to Hengshan, crossing nine Jiang, reaching Fushaoyuan.
s0102.idiomatic: From the southern slopes of Minshan to Hengshan, across the nine streams, to Fushaoyuan.

s0103.literal: He opened nine streams: weak water reaching Heli, surplus waves entering flowing sands.
s0103.idiomatic: He channeled nine rivers: the Weak to Heli, its tail into the shifting sands.

s0104.literal: He opened Black Water, reaching Three Perils, entering south sea.
s0104.idiomatic: He led the Black Water to Sanwei and out into the southern sea.

s0105.literal: He opened He Jishi, reaching Longmen, south reaching Huayin, east reaching Dizhu, again east reaching Mengjin, east crossing Luo's bend, reaching Dapi, north crossing Jiangshui, reaching Dalu, north spreading as nine He, together reverse He, entering sea.
s0105.idiomatic: He guided the Yellow River from Jishi and Longmen south to Huayin, east past the pillars at Mengjin, across the Luo bend to Dapi, north past the Jiang to the great alluvial plain, where it fanned into the nine channels and the backwash belt before reaching the sea.

s0106.literal: Bozhong opened Yang, east flowing became Han, again east became Canglang water, passed three rapids, entered Dabie, south entered Jiang, east collected marsh as Pengli, east became north Jiang, entered sea.
s0106.idiomatic: From Bozhong he opened the Han, turned it east through the Canglang reaches and three gorges to Dabie, then south into the Yangtze, east into Lake Pengli, and east again as the northern stream to the sea.

s0107.literal: Minshan opened Jiang, east divided became Tuo, again east reached Li, crossed nine Jiang, reached Dongling, east winding north met at Hui, east became middle Jiang, entered Mei.
s0107.idiomatic: From Minshan he split the Yangtze's eastern arm as the Tuo, led it past the Li reaches and the nine channels to Dongling, bent north to the confluence, and opened the middle stream to the sea.

s0108.literal: He opened Yan water, east became Ji, entered He, overflowed became Xing, east emerged north of Taoqiu, again east reached He, again northeast met Wen, again northeast entered sea.
s0108.idiomatic: He channeled the Yan into the Ji to the Yellow River, where it burst forth as the Xing pool, ran east from north of Taoqiu to the He marsh, then northeast by the Wen to the sea.

s0109.literal: He opened Huai from Tongbai, east met Si and Yi, east entered sea.
s0109.idiomatic: He traced the Huai from Tongbai east to join the Si and Yi, then to the sea.

s0110.literal: He opened Wei from Niaoshu same-burrow, east met Feng, again northeast reached Jing, east crossing Qi and Ju, entered He.
s0110.idiomatic: He opened the Wei from Niaoshu, east to the Feng, northeast to the Jing, then past the Qi and Ju into the Yellow River.

s0111.literal: He opened Luo from Xiong'er, northeast met Jian and Chan, again east met Yi, northeast entered He.
s0111.idiomatic: He led the Luo from Xiong'er northeast through the Jian and Chan, east to meet the Yi, and northeast into the Yellow River.

s0112.literal: Thereupon nine provinces together; four depths already dwelt; nine mountains cut and journeyed; nine streams washed source; nine marshes already banked; four seas met together.
s0112.idiomatic: Then the nine provinces were one domain; the four gulfs had homes; ranges were cleared; rivers scoured their beds; marshes were diked; and the four seas became one waterway.

s0113.literal: Six treasuries very cultivated; all soils mutually corrected; careful with wealth and levies; all modeled three soils completing revenue.
s0113.idiomatic: The six storehouses were stocked; soils were assayed; taxes were set with care; every grade of earth paid its due.

s0114.literal: Central state granted earth surnames: "Revere and put virtue first; do not resist my course."
s0114.idiomatic: The central lands received clan names and were told: "Honor virtue before all and do not defy my rule."

s0116.literal: He ordered beyond the Son of Heaven's domain five hundred li field-domain: hundred li tribute bound sheaves, two hundred li ears of grain, three hundred li straw service, four hundred li grain, five hundred li polished rice.
s0116.idiomatic: He fixed the five-hundred-li royal domain: the inner hundred li sent whole stalks, the next grain ears, the next fodder and labor, then millet, then rice at the rim.

s0117.literal: Field-domain outer five hundred li lord-domain: hundred li gathering, two hundred li duty states, three hundred li feudal lords.
s0117.idiomatic: Beyond that lay another five hundred li for the lords: inner districts for court estates, next for assigned service, outer for the feudal order.

s0118.literal: Lord-domain outer five hundred li peace-domain: three hundred li measure culture-teaching, two hundred li vigorous martial guard.
s0118.idiomatic: Outside that came the pacification belt: inner tracts for civil instruction, outer for military screen.

s0119.literal: Peace-domain outer five hundred li binding-domain: three hundred li yi, two hundred li cai.
s0119.idiomatic: Next was the coercive belt: inner Yi peoples, outer Cai settlements.

s0120.literal: Binding-domain outer five hundred li wild-domain: three hundred li man, two hundred li flow.
s0120.idiomatic: Outermost lay the wilds: inner Man tribes, outer exiles.

s0121.literal: East washed to sea, west covered to shifting sands, north and south reached: sound and teaching ended at four seas.
s0121.idiomatic: East to the ocean, west to the sands, north and south alike—his influence reached every shore.

s0122.literal: Thereupon the Emperor granted Yu dark jade tablet, thereby announcing success to all under Heaven.
s0122.idiomatic: The Emperor gave Yu a black scepter to proclaim the work finished to the realm.

s0123.literal: All under Heaven thereupon greatly peaceful order.
s0123.idiomatic: The realm then knew great peace.

s0124.literal: Gao Yao served as minister to regulate the people.
s0124.idiomatic: Gao Yao acted as judge to keep order among the people.

s0125.literal: Emperor Shun at court, Yu, Boyi, Gao Yao conversed with each other before the Emperor.
s0125.idiomatic: At Shun's audience Yu, Boyi, and Gao Yao debated policy before the throne.

s0126.literal: Gao Yao set forth his plan, saying, "Trust his virtue and way; counsel clear, assist harmony."
s0126.idiomatic: Gao Yao said, "Hold fast to true virtue and clear counsel, and harmony will follow."

s0127.literal: Yu said, "Yes—how shall we do it?"
s0127.idiomatic: Yu asked, "Agreed—but how do we put that into practice?"

s0129.literal: Careful with body cultivation, thinking long, generous ordering nine kin, many bright high wings, near can far in self.
s0129.idiomatic: Watch your own conduct, plan far ahead, cherish the nine branches of kin, lift up the worthy, and what is near in yourself will reach what is far.

s0130.literal: Yu bowed praising the words, saying, "Yes."
s0130.idiomatic: Yu bowed and said, "Well spoken—it is so."

s0132.literal: In knowing men, in pacifying the people.
s0132.idiomatic: Know your officers and secure the people.

s0134.literal: If all were thus, only the Emperor would find it hard.
s0134.idiomatic: If everyone did that, even a sage on the throne would find it hard.

s0135.literal: Knowing men then wise, able to appoint men;
s0135.idiomatic: To know men is wisdom and lets you place each in his post.

s0136.literal: able to pacify people then kind, black-haired people cherish him.
s0136.idiomatic: To pacify the people is kindness, and the common folk will cleave to you.

s0137.literal: Able to know able to kind, what worry about Huandou, what banish about Youmiao, what fear about clever words fine faces flatterers?
s0137.idiomatic: With both wisdom and kindness, why fear Huandou, why exile the Miao, why dread smooth talk and pretty faces?

s0139.literal: Also conduct has nine virtues; also speech has its virtue.
s0139.idiomatic: In conduct there are nine virtues; in words there are signs of them.

s0140.literal: Then saying, "Begin affairs: broad yet firm, soft yet standing, obedient yet respectful, ordered yet reverent, adaptable yet resolute, straight yet warm, simple yet incorrupt, hard yet solid, strong yet righteous—manifest their constancy—auspicious."
s0140.idiomatic: They are: to be generous yet disciplined, gentle yet firm, tractable yet dignified, orderly yet reverent, teachable yet resolute, candid yet mild, spare yet honest, stern yet true, forceful yet just. Show these as steady habits, and all will be well.

s0141.literal: Daily spread three virtues, early night assist bright having family.
s0141.idiomatic: Practice three virtues each day, morning and night, and you may govern a household.

s0142.literal: Daily strict shake reverence six virtues, bright gathering having state.
s0142.idiomatic: Strictly maintain six virtues with daily care, and you may rule a state.

s0143.literal: Receive together spread broadly, nine virtues all affairs, outstanding ones in office, hundred clerks reverent careful.
s0143.idiomatic: Welcome all talents, put the nine virtues to work, place the able in office, and every clerk will stand in awe.

s0144.literal: Do not teach deviant evil strange plots.
s0144.idiomatic: Never teach lewd ways or crooked schemes.

s0145.literal: Not its man dwelling its office—this is called disorder Heaven's affairs.
s0145.idiomatic: Put the wrong man in a post and you throw Heaven's business into chaos.

s0146.literal: Heaven punishes guilty; five punishments five applications.
s0146.idiomatic: Heaven punishes crime; the five penalties have their five uses.

s0147.literal: My words bottom can be practiced?
s0147.idiomatic: Can my words be carried out in practice?

s0148.literal: Yu said, "Your words reach can be traced in practice."
s0148.idiomatic: Yu said, "What you say can be carried out and will show results."

s0149.literal: Gao Yao said, "I have no knowledge; I think only to assist the way."
s0149.idiomatic: Gao Yao said, "I know little myself—I only wish to help along the right path."

s0151.literal: Emperor Shun told Yu, "You also speak expansively."
s0151.idiomatic: Emperor Shun said to Yu, "You speak plainly in your turn."

s0153.idiomatic: What more is there to say!

s0154.idiomatic: Day and night I have only labored without rest.

s0155.literal: Gao Yao pressed Yu saying, "What is called diligent diligent?"
s0155.idiomatic: Gao Yao pressed him: "What do you mean by striving without ceasing?"

s0156.literal: Yu said, Vast flood swelled heaven, vastly embraced mountains overflowed hills, lower people all submerged in water.
s0156.idiomatic: Yu answered: When the flood rose to the sky, wrapped the hills and drowned the valleys, the people were trapped in the waters.

s0157.literal: I land rode carriage, water rode boat, mud rode sledge, mountain rode litter, marched mountains cut trees.
s0157.idiomatic: I rode a carriage on dry ground, boats on the water, sledges in mud, litters in the hills, and cut paths through the forests.

s0158.literal: Together with Yi gave multitude rice fresh food.
s0158.idiomatic: With Yi I gave the people grain and fresh meat from the marshes.

s0159.literal: To decide nine streams reach four seas, dredged ditches channels reach them streams.
s0159.idiomatic: I opened the nine rivers to the four seas and deepened the field channels into the trunk streams.

s0160.literal: Together with Ji gave multitude hard-to-obtain food.
s0160.idiomatic: With Ji I supplied food wherever famine struck.

s0161.literal: Food scarce, adjusted surplus mending shortage, moved dwellings.
s0161.idiomatic: Where grain ran short I shifted surplus to cover want and resettled the people.

s0162.literal: Multitude people then settled, myriad states for order.
s0162.idiomatic: Then the masses found a home and the myriad regions were governed.

s0163.idiomatic: Gao Yao said, "Truly—this is the finest of policies."

s0166.literal: Careful with your occupying the throne, secure your resting.
s0166.idiomatic: Watch how you sit the throne and keep your footing steady.

s0167.literal: Assist virtue, all under Heaven greatly respond.
s0167.idiomatic: Support true virtue, and the whole realm will answer you.

s0168.literal: Clear mind thereby bright await Supreme God's commands; Heaven will again mandate use blessing.
s0168.idiomatic: With a clear heart wait on Heaven's command, and Heaven will renew its blessing.

s0169.idiomatic: The Emperor said: O ministers, ministers!

s0170.literal: Ministers are my thighs arms eyes ears.
s0170.idiomatic: You are my legs, arms, eyes, and ears.

s0171.literal: I desire assist have people, you assist them.
s0171.idiomatic: I wish to help the people—aid me in it.

s0172.literal: I desire observe ancient men's images.
s0172.idiomatic: I wish to read the pattern of the ancients.

s0173.literal: Sun moon stars, make patterns embroidered colors, you clarify them.
s0173.idiomatic: Sun, moon, and stars should pattern robes and insignia—you shall set them clear.

s0174.literal: I desire hear six pipes five tones eight sounds, come begin smooth, to go out enter five words, you listen.
s0174.idiomatic: I would hear the six pitch-pipes, five notes, and eight timbres in harmony—you shall attune them.

s0175.literal: I if biased, you correct assist me.
s0175.idiomatic: If I go astray, correct and steady me.

s0176.idiomatic: Do not flatter me to my face.

s0177.idiomatic: Nor slander me behind my back.

s0179.literal: As for many slander favorite ministers, lord virtue sincerely spread all clear.
s0179.idiomatic: If slanderers and favorites abound, let your true virtue shine through and all will be clear.

s0181.literal: Emperor if not timely, spread same good evil then no merit.
s0181.idiomatic: If the Son of Heaven is out of step and spreads the same measure to good and evil alike, we shall accomplish nothing.

s0183.literal: Emperor said, Do not be like Danzhu arrogant, only slow wandering is his love, only water go boat, flock licentious in house, use cut off his generations.
s0183.idiomatic: The Emperor said: Do not be like Danzhu, proud and idle, who boated for pleasure, debauched his household, and lost his line.

s0184.idiomatic: I cannot follow such a course.

s0185.literal: Yu said, "I marry Tushan on xinren, guijia, bore Qi I not treat as son, thereby able complete earth-water merit.
s0185.idiomatic: Yu said, "I wed a woman of Tushan after four days away; when our son Qi was born I did not dote on him—thus I finished the flood work."

s0186.literal: Assist complete five domains, reaching five thousand li, twelve divisions per province, outer thin four seas, all establish five chiefs, each guide having merit.
s0186.idiomatic: I helped shape the five zones out to five thousand li, set twelve divisions in each province, reached to the rim of the seas, appointed five regional overseers, and urged every district to earn its keep.

s0187.literal: Miao stubborn not approach merit, Emperor should think on them.
s0187.idiomatic: Only the Miao remained stubborn—think of them, my lord.

s0188.literal: Emperor said, "Conducting my virtue is your ordering the merit."
s0188.idiomatic: The Emperor answered, "It is your ordering of merit that spreads my virtue."

s0190.literal: Gao Yao thereupon revered Yu's virtue, ordered people all model Yu.
s0190.idiomatic: Gao Yao then honored Yu's example and told the people to imitate him.

s0191.literal: If not matching words, punishment follows them.
s0191.idiomatic: Those who defied his commands faced the law.

s0192.idiomatic: Shun's virtue shone the brighter for it.

s0193.literal: Thereupon Kui performed music, ancestors arrived, host of lords yielding to each other, birds beasts soaring dance, xiaoshao nine completions, phoenixes came pattern, hundred beasts lead dance, hundred officials truly harmonious.
s0193.idiomatic: Then Kui played the Nine Shao; the shades of the ancestors drew near; the lords yielded to one another; birds and beasts danced; phoenixes appeared; every creature joined the measure, and the officers moved in perfect accord.

s0194.literal: Emperor using this made song, saying, "Ascend Heaven's mandate, only season only danger."
s0194.idiomatic: The Emperor made a song: "Heaven's charge is high—mind the seasons and the perilous moments."

s0195.literal: Then sang, "Thighs arms joyful! Head rises! Hundred crafts bright!"
s0195.idiomatic: He sang, "Joy to our limbs! Awake, our head! Brightly thrive all crafts!"

s0196.literal: Gao Yao bowing hand touching head raised saying, "Think! Lead to raise affairs, careful your model, reverent!"
s0196.idiomatic: Gao Yao bowed and cried, "Remember this! Act to raise new works; watch your pattern; be reverent!"

s0197.literal: Then changed making song, saying, "Head bright! Thighs arms good! Multitude matters peaceful!"
s0197.idiomatic: He sang in turn, "The head is wise! The limbs are good! All affairs are at peace!"

s0198.literal: (Shun) again sang, "Head cluttered! Thighs arm lazy! Myriad matters ruin!"
s0198.idiomatic: Shun sang again, "If the head is muddled, the limbs grow slack, and every task collapses!"

s0199.idiomatic: The Emperor bowed and said, "Yes—go and be reverent!"

s0200.literal: Thereupon all under Heaven all took as model Yu's bright measures numbers sound music, for mountains rivers spirit lord.
s0200.idiomatic: All the realm then took Yu's calendrics, measures, and music as their model and made him lord of hills and streams.

s0201.literal: Emperor Shun recommended Yu to Heaven, as heir.
s0201.idiomatic: Emperor Shun presented Yu to Heaven as successor.

s0202.literal: Seventeen years then Emperor Shun passed away.
s0202.idiomatic: Seventeen years later Emperor Shun died.

s0203.literal: Three years mourning finished, Yu declined yielding to Shun's son Shangjun at Yangcheng.
s0203.idiomatic: When the three years' mourning ended, Yu withdrew to Yangcheng to yield to Shun's son Shangjun.

s0204.literal: All under Heaven lords all left Shangjun and courted Yu.
s0204.idiomatic: The lords of the realm forsook Shangjun and turned to Yu.

s0205.literal: Yu thereupon then assumed Son of Heaven position, south faced all under Heaven, state title said Xia hou, surname Si clan.
s0205.idiomatic: Yu then took the throne, faced south over the realm, styled his dynasty Xia, and took the clan name Si.

s0206.literal: Emperor Yu established and raised Gao Yao recommending him, and was about to transfer government, but Gao Yao died.
s0206.idiomatic: When Emperor Yu came to power he promoted Gao Yao and meant to hand him the government, but Gao Yao died.

s0207.literal: He enfeoffed Gao Yao's posterity at Ying and Liu, or at Xu.
s0207.idiomatic: He enfeoffed Gao Yao's descendants at Ying, Liu, and perhaps Xu.

s0208.literal: Then afterward raised Yi, entrusted him government.
s0208.idiomatic: Later he raised Yi and entrusted him with administration.

s0209.literal: Ten years, Emperor Yu east toured inspection, reaching Kuaiji then passed away.
s0209.idiomatic: In the tenth year of his reign Yu toured east to Kuaiji and died there.

s0210.literal: He gave all under Heaven to Yi.
s0210.idiomatic: He left the realm to Yi.

s0211.literal: Three years mourning finished, Yi yielded to Emperor Yu's son Qi, and declined dwelling south of Mount Ji.
s0211.idiomatic: After the mourning Yi yielded to Yu's son Qi and withdrew south of Mount Ji.

s0212.literal: Yu's son Qi worthy, all under Heaven fixed intent on him.
s0212.idiomatic: Yu's son Qi was worthy, and the realm's hopes settled on him.

s0213.literal: When Yu passed away, although he gave Yi, Yi's assisting Yu days shallow, all under Heaven not harmonious.
s0213.idiomatic: Though Yu had named Yi, Yi had served at Yu's side too briefly to win the people's trust.

s0214.literal: Therefore lords all left Yi and courted Qi, saying, "Our lord is Emperor Yu's son."
s0214.idiomatic: So the lords left Yi for Qi, saying, "He is our lord, the Son of Yu."

s0215.literal: Thereupon Qi then assumed Son of Heaven position, this is Xia hou Emperor Qi.
s0215.idiomatic: Qi then mounted the throne as Emperor Qi of Xia.

s0216.literal: Xia hou Emperor Qi, Yu's son, his mother a woman of the Tushan clan.
s0216.idiomatic: Emperor Qi of Xia was Yu's son; his mother was a lady of Tushan.

s0217.literal: Youhu clan not submitting, Qi attacked them, great battle at Gan.
s0217.idiomatic: When the Youhu refused submission, Qi attacked and fought the great battle of Gan.

s0218.literal: About to battle, made Gan oath, then summoned six ministers declaring it.
s0218.idiomatic: Before the fight he issued the Oath of Gan and called the six ministers to hear it.

s0220.literal: Six affairs people, I oath tell you: Youhu clan threaten insult five phases, neglect abandon three rectifiers, Heaven uses extirpate cut off its mandate.
s0220.idiomatic: You who command the six hosts, I swear this to you: Youhu has insulted the Five Agents, spurned the Three Beginnings—Heaven therefore ends their mandate.

s0221.literal: Now I only together conduct Heaven's punishment.
s0221.idiomatic: I now join you in executing Heaven's sentence.

s0222.literal: Left not attacking at left, right not attacking at right, you not together mandate.
s0222.idiomatic: Those on the left who fail the left wing, those on the right who fail the right—you disobey the command.

s0223.literal: Charioteers not their horses' regulation, you not together mandate.
s0223.idiomatic: Charioteers who cannot master their teams—you likewise disobey.

s0224.literal: Obeying mandate, rewarded at the ancestor;
s0224.idiomatic: Whoever obeys will be rewarded before the ancestral shrine.

s0225.literal: not obeying mandate, executed at the earth altar; I will put you and your kin to death.
s0225.idiomatic: Whoever disobeys will be slain at the soil altar, you and your households with you.

s0228.literal: Xia hou Emperor Qi passed away, son Emperor Taikang established.
s0228.idiomatic: When Emperor Qi died, his son Taikang succeeded.

s0229.literal: Emperor Taikang lost state, five elder brothers, waited at Luo's bend, made Song of the Five Sons.
s0229.idiomatic: Taikang lost the throne; five brothers waited at the Luo bend and composed the Song of the Five Sons.

s0230.literal: Taikang passed away, younger brother Zhongkang established, this is Emperor Zhongkang.
s0230.idiomatic: Taikang was succeeded by his brother Zhongkang, known as Emperor Zhongkang.

s0231.literal: Emperor Zhongkang's time, Xi and He drowned in debauchery, abandoned seasons disordered days.
s0231.idiomatic: Under Zhongkang the Xi and He stewards sank into vice and threw the calendar into confusion.

s0232.literal: Yin went punishing them, made Yin expedition.
s0232.idiomatic: Yin marched against them in the Punitive Expedition of Yin.

s0233.literal: Zhongkang passed away, son Emperor Xiang established.
s0233.idiomatic: Zhongkang died and his son Xiang ruled.

s0234.literal: Emperor Xiang passed away, son Emperor Shaokang established.
s0234.idiomatic: Xiang died and his son Shaokang ruled.

s0235.literal: Emperor Shaokang passed away, son Emperor Zhu established.
s0235.idiomatic: Shaokang died and his son Zhu ruled.

s0236.literal: Emperor Zhu passed away, son Emperor Huai established.
s0236.idiomatic: Zhu died and his son Huai ruled.

s0237.literal: Emperor Huai passed away, son Emperor Mang established.
s0237.idiomatic: Huai died and his son Mang ruled.

s0238.literal: Emperor Mang passed away, son Emperor Xie established.
s0238.idiomatic: Mang died and his son Xie ruled.

s0239.literal: Emperor Xie passed away, son Emperor Bujiang established.
s0239.idiomatic: Xie died and his son Bujiang ruled.

s0240.literal: Emperor Bujiang passed away, younger brother Emperor Jiong established.
s0240.idiomatic: Bujiang died and his brother Jiong ruled.

s0241.literal: Emperor Jiong passed away, son Emperor Jin established.
s0241.idiomatic: Jiong died and his son Jin ruled.

s0242.literal: Emperor Jin passed away, established Emperor Bujiang's son Kongjia, this is Emperor Kongjia.
s0242.idiomatic: Jin died and Bujiang's son Kongjia took the throne as Emperor Kongjia.

s0243.literal: Emperor Kongjia established, loved methods ghosts spirits, affairs dissolute chaotic.
s0243.idiomatic: Kongjia turned to occult rites and sank into disorder.

s0244.literal: Xia hou virtue declined, lords rebelled them.
s0244.idiomatic: Xia's virtue waned and the lords rebelled.

s0245.literal: Heaven sent down dragons two, having male female, Kongjia could not feed them, not yet obtained Dragon-herd clan.
s0245.idiomatic: Heaven sent down a pair of dragons, but Kongjia could not feed them and had no Dragon-keepers.

s0246.literal: Taotang already declined, afterward had Liu Lei, learned tame dragons at Dragon-herd clan, to serve Kongjia.
s0246.idiomatic: When the house of Taotang waned, a man named Liu Lei learned dragon-tending from the Dragon-keepers and served Kongjia.

s0247.literal: Kongjia granted him surname said Imperial Dragon clan, received Pig-wei's posterity.
s0247.idiomatic: Kongjia gave him the clan name Yinglong and the fief of the Pig-Wei line.

s0248.literal: Dragons one female died, to feed Xia hou.
s0248.idiomatic: When the female dragon died, he served its flesh to the Xia court.

s0249.literal: Xia hou sent seek, feared then moved away.
s0249.idiomatic: When Xia demanded more, he fled in terror.

s0250.literal: Kongjia passed away, son Emperor Gao established.
s0250.idiomatic: Kongjia died and his son Gao ruled.

s0251.literal: Emperor Gao passed away, son Emperor Fa established.
s0251.idiomatic: Gao died and his son Fa ruled.

s0252.literal: Emperor Fa passed away, son Emperor Lügui established, this is Jie.
s0252.idiomatic: Fa died and his son Lügui became the tyrant Jie.

s0253.literal: Emperor Jie's time, from Kongjia onward many lords rebelled Xia, Jie not pursuing virtue but martially injuring hundred surnames, hundred surnames not endure.
s0253.idiomatic: Under Jie, lords had rebelled since Kongjia's day; Jie cultivated force, not virtue, until the people could bear no more.

s0254.literal: Then summoned Tang and imprisoned him at Xia tower, afterward released him.
s0254.idiomatic: He summoned Tang, jailed him in the Xia tower, then let him go.

s0255.literal: Tang cultivated virtue, lords all returned Tang, Tang thereupon led troops to attack Xia Jie.
s0255.idiomatic: Tang built up his virtue until the lords rallied to him, then led an army against Jie.

s0256.literal: Jie fled Mingtiao, thereupon banished then died.
s0256.idiomatic: Jie fled to Mingtiao, was driven out, and died.

s0257.literal: Jie told people saying, "I regret not then killing Tang at Xia tower, causing arrive here."
s0257.idiomatic: Jie said to those about him, "I rue not killing Tang in the Xia tower when I could—now I am brought to this pass."

s0258.literal: Tang thereupon trod Son of Heaven position, replacing Xia facing all under Heaven.
s0258.idiomatic: Tang then took the throne in Xia's place and faced the realm as Son of Heaven.

s0259.literal: Tang enfeoffed Xia's posterity, reaching Zhou enfeoffed at Qi.
s0259.idiomatic: Tang enfeoffed Xia's descendants; by Zhou times they held the state of Qi.

s0260.literal: The Grand Scribe says: Yu bore the Si surname; afterward divided enfeoffments, used states as surnames, therefore there were Xia hou, Youhu, Younan, Zhenxun, Tongcheng, Bao, Fei, Qi, Zeng, Xin, Ming, Zhen, Ge clans.
s0260.idiomatic: The Grand Scribe remarks: Yu's line took the name Si; later branches adopted their states as surnames, producing houses such as Xiahou, Youhu, Younan, Zhenxun, Tongcheng, Bao, Fei, Qi, Zeng, Xin, Ming, Zhen, and Ge.

s0261.literal: Confucius corrected Xia calendar, scholars many transmit Xia minor calendar saying.
s0261.idiomatic: Confucius set the Xia calendar straight, and scholars still pass down the Xia minor calendar.

s0262.literal: From Yu and Xia time, tribute levies already complete.
s0262.idiomatic: From the age of Yu and Xia the tribute system was already full-formed.

s0263.literal: Some say Yu met lords south of the river, reckoned merit then passed away, thereby buried there, commanded called Kuaiji.
s0263.idiomatic: Some hold that Yu met the lords south of the Yangtze, settled accounts of merit, and died there—hence the name Kuaiji, "gathering of tallies."
